main_event: Crew of 78 Air Transport Squadron aircraft observed round, pea-sized greenish yellow object with long greenish yellow tail covering 10 degrees of arc in straight and level flight for approximately 30 seconds at 0702Z on 18 December 1957, position 38°36'N 12°47'W (west of Gibraltar).
sighted_object: round; size of pea; greenish yellow; long greenish yellow tail covering approximately 10 degrees of arc; straight and level flight; blocked from view by left rudder; bearing from 355 to 185 degrees at 20 degrees above horizon
witness_description: Captain William G. Gattrs (pilot, AC 21 L 72, 78 Air Transport Squadron, Charleston SC); Lt. Donald L. Hall (co-pilot); AWZC Betty H. Gnlas (flight attendant); aircraft magnetic heading 100 degrees, TAS 250 knots, above all clouds
interesting_points: Field intelligence officer suggested 'possible this is sighting of Para Three Missile' in paragraph eleven
AFCIN-4F3 (Maj. Barnes, 18 Mar 58) confirmed: 'No known guided missile test range exist in this area and no operation involving missiles have ever been reported in the area'
ATIC record card: 'In view of information given and other sources, no missile ever reported in this area. Sighting very possibly astronomical meteor'
Handwritten analyst note: '30 seconds after each. That it was a meteor. check slews, no missile rotation — last one'
Aircraft above all clouds; weather unlimited; winds aloft 305/25
CSV flag C:dur_meta=600.0s_vs_form=60.0s was haiku error; form clearly states '30 seconds'
